content.full_text:
Fern PolrrrcAt- Pnacrrcss CoMMrssroN
.128 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
January 22,2010
Roger W. Anderson
3ll Dickens Way
Santa Cfltz, California 95064
Re
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-09-281
Dear Mr. Anderson:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note thai our advice is based solely
on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of
otherconflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Govemment Code
Section 1090.
QUESTTON
As a member of the Local Agency Formation Commission of Santa Cruz County, may
you vote on an application by the City of Santa Cruz (the "City") to add certain real property to
the City's sphere of influence and an application by the University of Califomia (the
"University") to receive water and sewer services from the City to a portion of real property
owned by the University located outside the city limits?
CONCLUSION
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions do not prohibit you from voting on either
application. It is not reasonably foreseeable that the decisions will have a material financial
effect on any of your economic interests.
' The Politi.rl Retbrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections ll 1000 through 9101.4. All statutory
ret'erences are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Califbrnia Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.


File No. 4-09-281
Page No. 2
FACTS
You are a commissioner of the Local Agency Formation Commission of Santa Cruz
County ("LAFCO"). Two applications are pending before LAFCO relating to real property
owned by the Regents of the University on which the University of California Santa Cruz
("UCSC") campus is located. The University is a government agency. Application No. 928,
submitted by the City, seeks to amend the City's sphere of influence to inciude certain real
property (Assessor's Parcel Numbers 061-321-40 and062-041-49) located on the northern
portion of the UCSC campus. Application 929, filedby the University, seeks to receive water
and sewer services from the City to a portion of the campus located outside the City limits. The
applications concern the University's Long Range Development Plan (the "plan") which
contemplates developing an area known as the North Campus. The North Campus is currently
undeveloped and is outside the City limits. Following the commencement of litigation between,
among others, the City and the University regarding the Plan, the litigation was settled. The
settlement agreement sets forth limits on future enrollment and increises in traffic at the UCSC
campus. It also requires the University to provide minimum housing units based on enrollment
and provides for payment of fees by the University based on increased water usage.
You and your spouse own a principal residence located on the UCSC campus. you own
the house, but you lease the lot from the Regents of the University of California pursuant to a 60
year prepaid lease. The parcel on which your home is located is in the southern portion of the
UCSC campus and is within the City limits. Your house is in a faculty housing area with 130
owner-occupied units. Eleven of the units, including your own, are custom homes and the others
are town houses and condominiums. The minimum distance between your residence and the
area under consideration by LAFCO is approximately 7,600 feet. Youare also a faculty member
At UCSC.
You ask whether the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act bar you from voting on the
two LAFCO applications.
AI\ALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section Sl00l(b).) Section g7100
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a govemmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.
The Commission has adopted an eighrstep standard analysis for deciding whether an
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The geneial rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a govemmental
decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one o, *6r" of his or her
financial interests.


File No. 4-09-281
Page No. 3
step 1. Are you a "public official" within the meaning of section g7100?
Section 82048 defines a public official as "every member, officer, employee or consultant
of a state or local government agency." As a member of LAFCO, which is a iocal government
agency,' you are a public official. Therefore, you may not make, participate in making, or
otherwise use your position to make, participate in making or influence any decisions that will
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of your economic interests.
Step 2. Will you be making, participating in making or influencing a governmental
decision?
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official "participates in a governmental decision" when,
acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or
intervening review, the official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the
decisionmaker regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decisi-on if, for the
purpose of influencing the decision, the official contacts or appears before or otherwise attempts
to influence, any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation
18702.3.)
By participating in LAFCO's vote on the two applications, you will be making or
participating in two governmental decisions.
Step 3. What are your economic interests?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising
from certain enumerated economic interests. These economic interests are described in Section
87103 and Regulations 18703-18703.5, inclusive:
o { public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he
or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a); Regulation
18703.1(a).)
o d public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
(Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703. l(b).)
o ,{ public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she
has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 37103(b); Regulation 1g703.2.)
2 "Local government agency" means a county, city or district of any kind including sshool district, or any
other local or regional political subdivision, or any department, division, buriau, office, boaid, commission or othir
agency ofthe foregoing (Section 82041).


File No. 4-09-281
Page No.4
o An official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87 103(c); Regulation I 8703.3.)
o I public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her
if the gifts total $420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e);
Regulation 18703.4.)
o A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is commonly
referred to as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)
Real Prooern
As co-owner of your home, in which you presumably have a greater than $2,000
investment, and a lessee of the land on which your home is located,3 you have an economic
interest in the real property.
Source of Income
As a faculty member of USCS, you will, presumably, have been paid by the University
$500 or more within 12 months prior to LAFCO's decision regarding the University's
application. However, the Act defines "income" to exclude salary and benefit payments from a
state government agency, such as the University. (Section 87103, Regulation 18703.5.) Thus,
the University is not a source of income to you.
Personal Finances
A public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal finances.
(Regulation 18703.5.) A govemmental decision will have an effect on this economic interest if
the decision will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official, or
his or her immediate family, increasing or decreasing. (Ibid.)
Step 4. Will your economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in decisions you will
make, participate in making or influence as a public official?
Real Property
Under Section 18704.2(a)(l), real property is directly involved in a governmental
decision if:
"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of that real
property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries)
3 "Interestinreal property"includesanyleaseholdinrealpropertylocatedinthejurisclictionifthefair
market value of the interest is $2,000 or more. (Section 82033.)


File No. .{-09-281
Page No. 5
of the property which is the subject of the govemmental decision. For purposes of
subdivision (aX5), real property is located iwithin 500 feet of the bouniaries 1or
proposed boundaries) of the real property which is the subject of the
govemmental decision' if any part of the real property is within 500 feet of the
boundaries (or proposed boundaries) ofthe redevilopment project area.
"(2)The govemmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning, annexation or
deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or exclusi-on from any city,
county, district.or other local govemmental subdivision, of the real property in
which the official has an interest or a similar decision affecting the ieai properry.
For purposes of this subdivision, the terms "zoning" and "reziring,' shall refer to
the act of establishing or changing the zoning or lind use designatlo, on the real
property in which the official has an interest-
"(3) The govemmental decision involves the issuance, denial or revocation of a
license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a specific use or uses of
the real property in which the official has an interest.
"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal or modification of
any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the reai property in which the official
has an interest.
"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to select the
project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project area committee, to
certify the environmental document, to adopt the redeveiopment plan, to add
territory to the redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend any of the above
decisions; an$ reaf property in which the official has an interest, or any part of it
is located within the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the 
J '
redevelopment area.
"(6) The decision involves construction of, or improvements to, streets, water,
sewer' storm drainage or similar facilities, and the real property in which the
official has an interest will receive new or improved services.,,
lecause your property does not fall within any of the foregoing categories, it is deemed to be
indirectly involved. If a public official's economic interesi is not dir"ectly involved in a
governmental decision, it is indirectly involved. (Regulation 1g704(a).)
Personal Finances
Regulation 18704.5 provides that: "A public official or his or her immediate family are
deemed tobe direcrly involved in a governmertal decision which has orryfinancial effect on his
or her personal finances or those of his or her immediate family." (Emphasis added.)


File No. 4-09-281
Page No. 6
Step 5. What is the applicable materiality standard?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a governmental decision on a pubiic official's ecooomic interest is material. (Regulation
18700(a)') Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the nature of the economic interest
and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involveiin the agency,s decision.
Real Prooern
The financial effect of a govemmental decision on real property that is indirectly
involved in the governmental decision is presumed not to be material. This presumption may be
rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the gor"--"ntal decision, its
financial effect, and the nature of the real property whicf,makelt ,ealonabty ior"r".oule that the
decision will have a material financial effect on the real property in which the public official has
an interest. (Regulation 18705.2(b)(1).) Examples of such specific circumstances include the
development potential or income producing poiential of the ieal property, the use of the real
property and the character of the neighborhood, including substantial efiects on traffic, view,
privacy, intensity of use, noise levels and air emissions.
You state that the Plan area is located in the northem portion of the campus and your
home is located in the southern portion. Certain provisions of tt, Settlement Ag.""-.nt urd
statements made in one of the LAFCO letters, such as the assumption that enrollment and traffic
will increase under the Plan and providing water to the PIan area could affect water supplies
throughout the City during a drought, rrgg.rt that the Plan, if implemented, may have some
impact on the campus as a whole, including your property. (For lxample, your property could
experience increased traffic and decreased water *piti"r.; Because the plan cannot be
'implemented in its culrent form without LAFCo's aip.or.at of the University,s application,
LAFCo's decision could affect the University's expansion plans. We believe, however, that
these facts are insufficient to rebut the presumptiorthat the effects of the LAFC9 decision on
your property will not be material.
Personal Finances
Regulation 1 8705.5 provides:
"A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's personal finances
is marerial if it is at least $ 250 in.rny 12 month period. when determining
whether a governmental decision has a material iinancial effect on a public
official's economic interest in his or her personal finances, neither a financial
effect on the,value of real property o*r"d directly or indirectly by the official, nor
a financial effect on the gross revenues, expenses, or value ofassets and liabilities
of a business entity in which the official hai a direct or indirect investment
interest shall be considered.,'


File No. 4-09-281
Page No. 7
Accordingly, any financial effect on yourinterest in your real property is not analyzed
separately under the "personal financial effects" rule.
Step 6' Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of a governmental decision on
your economic interests will meet the applicable materiality starirlard?
An effect is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if the effect is ..substantially
likely." (Regulation 18706; In re Thorner (1975) I FppC ops. 19g.) A financial effect need not
be a certainty to be considered reasonably foreseeable. on the othei hand, if J;if;;.;;r, 
;'
mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. As stated above, it is not reasonably
foreseeable that LAFCo decisions regarding your real property interest will have a material
financial effect on that interest. Therefore, your participation in the LAFCo decisions will not
violate the Act's confl ict-of-interest provisions.
Steps 7 & 8: The "public generally" and "legally required participation, exceptions.
Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is
reasonably foreseeable, he or she still may not-be disqualified if the financial effect of the
governmental decision on the public official's ""onorni. interest is indistinguishable from
its e^ffect ol the public generally (section 87103, Regulations 1g700(b)(7) and 18707(a)),
or if the official is legally required to participate (Seition 87103; Regulation lg70g). you
have not presented any facts indicating that ;ithe; of these exceptioni is applicable to
your situation.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division
vJjgl

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: As a member of the Local Agency Formation Commission of Santa Cruz County, may
you vote on an application by the City of Santa Cruz (the "City") to add certain real property to
the City's sphere of influence and an application by the University of Califomia (the
"University") to receive water and sewer services from the City to a portion of real property
owned by the University located outside the city limits?

CONCLUSION: The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions do not prohibit you from voting on either
application. It is not reasonably foreseeable that the decisions will have a material financial
effect on any of your economic interests.

regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Califbrnia Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

Anderson 3ll Dickens Way Santa Cfltz, California 95064 Re Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-09-281 Dear Mr. Anderson: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note thai our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of otherconflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Govemment Code Section 1090. QUESTTON As a member of the Local Agency Formation Commission of Santa Cruz County, may you vote on an application by the City of Santa Cruz (the "City") to add certain real property to the City's sphere of influence and an application by the University of Califomia (the "University") to receive water and sewer services from the City to a portion of real property owned by the University located outside the city limits? CONCLUSION The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions do not prohibit you from voting on either application. It is not reasonably foreseeable that the decisions will have a material financial effect on any of your economic interests. ' The Politi.rl Retbrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections ll 1000 through 9101.4. All statutory ret'erences are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Califbrnia Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. 4-09-281 Page No. 2 FACTS You are a commissioner of the Local Agency Formation Commission of Santa Cruz County ("LAFCO"). Two applications are pending before LAFCO relating to real property owned by the Regents of the University on which the University of California Santa Cruz ("UCSC") campus is located. The University is a government agency. Application No. 928, submitted by the City, seeks to amend the City's sphere of influence to inciude certain real property (Assessor's Parcel Numbers 061-321-40 and062-041-49) located on the northern portion of the UCSC campus. Application 929, filedby the University, seeks to receive water and sewer services from the City to a portion of the campus located outside the City limits. The applications concern the University's Long Range Development Plan (the "plan") which contemplates developing an area known as the North Campus. The North Campus is currently undeveloped and is outside the City limits. Following the commencement of litigation between, among others, the City and the University regarding the Plan, the litigation was settled. The settlement agreement sets forth limits on future enrollment and increises in traffic at the UCSC campus. It also requires the University to provide minimum housing units based on enrollment and provides for payment of fees by the University based on increased water usage. You and your spouse own a
